# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Most Disturbing Film EVER?

**Score:** 51
**Body:** What's the most Disturbing/Violent/Gore and Psychologically Traumatic film that you have ever watched. That gave you Shivers in your spine and nightmare thinking about how disturbing the visuals were.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. We need to talk about Kevin…..

Try it out if you haven’t seen it. It’s one of those movies you will just sit there in the quiet after it ends asking yourself wtf…. Just wtf 
2. Happiness (1998)
  - I saw that in the cinema back in the day on a friggin date! We had no idea what it was about, just decided to watch whatever was on.


We didn't have sex that night.
3. Irreversible 
4. Requiem for A Dream messed me up. I watched it in HS after smoking pot with friends. I never touched a hard drug after that movie. 
  - Ditto. Ellen Burstyn's performance/depiction opened my eyes to how cruel this world is. Especially the force feed scene.

Though I question whether "Kids" disturbed/scared me more...
5. Kids
6. Dear Zachary
7. Dear Zachary for me
8. man bites dog 

and it’s not even close, a very far off second would be salo 

## 2. My 4 year old has been repeatedly asking to watch "something scary"

**Score:** 37
**Body:** No idea why, she's never watched anything remotely scary before. I'm thinking about Paranorman or Monster House, but does anyone know of anything that won't give a 4 year old nightmares but is a little spooky?

Edit: Thank you all so much for the suggestions!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Monsters, Inc. (2001)
  - It has to be this. There's even a little girl that represents her. The character is scared, your little girl's scared. Character overcomes her fear and falls in love with the monsters, yours does too.

It's easily the safest option to not give her nightmares.
2. Mom of 3 big weenies here, their favorites "scary" movies around that age: 

Curious George Halloween Boo Fest  
A Nightmare Before Christmas  
Monsters Inc  
The Land Before Time  
Monster House  
Megamind  
Monsters vs. Aliens  
Despicable Me  
Hotel Transylvania  
Home  
Shreck  
Disney's Ichabod and Mr. Toad  
Dumbo  
Fantasia  
Peter and the Wolf  


Movies they found too scary, even now:

The Secret of NIMH  
The Black Cauldron   
James and the Giant Peach  
Frankenweenie  
Spirited Away (that was on me, they were too young)  
Anything with unwanted transformation scenes: Luca, Turning Red, the donkey scene in Pinocchio


  - James and the Giant Peach is legit freaky!
3. Casper.
4. I was likely a big wimp but I was scared of the T-Rex in Land Before Time when I was a kid. Haha.

Coraline and Corpse Bride are probably too scary but possibly Nightmare Before Christmas and James and the Giant Peach may not be?
5. Event Horizon 

/s
6. Addams Family movies
7. Nightmare Before Christmas?
8. escape to witch mountain - old Disney 



## 3. Looking for a genuinely terrifying horror movie for movie night.

**Score:** 26
**Body:** Looking for a genuinely terrifying horror movie for movie night. Not just creepy or slow-burn. I want something that will absolutely wreck my sleep tonight.

Preferably a film with intense atmosphere, brutal jump scares, and that lingering dread that sticks with you long after the credits roll. The kind where you’re suddenly suspicious of every shadow in your room.

What’s the scariest movie you’ve ever watched that actually made it hard to sleep after?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Event Horizon
2. Not the scariest, but go with "The Wailing"
3. [Rec]
4. For me it's Smile and Smile 2. A decent mix of jumpscares, uneasiness and dread. Last time a film scared me like this was the Ring.....that was 24 years ago.
  - I saw Smile 2 in an empty theater (1st showing on a Tuesday) and it was *so* gloriously creepy being in that huge dark theater by myself! After it was over I was practically running out of that theater trying to just get back to a place that had lights and people. 

It's one of the best horror movie experiences I've ever had. A great movie was made even better by a really creepy atmosphere. 

...

Edit: word
5. Session 9. ALL the lights out in your place, crank it up. Immerse yourself.
6. Kill List 

The Babadook 

Exorcist 

The Ring (Japanese version)  
7. The Ritual. 
8. Original Spanish [Rec] if you CRT TV watch it on that 
9. When Evil Lurks genuinely terrified me.

Any horrible evil thing that indiscriminately destroys your loved ones.

## 4. Need some more “mind fuck” movies

**Score:** 16
**Body:** fight club is my favorite movie followed by Donnie Darko. I also really really enjoy memento and shutter island. more like these please but specifically the first two!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You’ll definitely like Coherence 
2. Lost Highway (1997)
3. Primal Fear (1996) is the first one that comes to my mind every time, but maybe I'm biased because Edward Norton is one of my favorite actors of all time


4. Frailty

Stir of echoes 

Identity 

Vanilla sky

The game

Fallen

Inception

The usual suspects 

Lucky number Slevin

The butterfly effect 

Mullholland drive

Seven

The bone collector

Gothika

Disturbia 

Hard candy

The life of David Gale

Double jeopardy
5. Jacobs Ladder (1990)  Pi (1998)
6. Triangle
7. Of course the Sixth Sense
8. The Skeleton Key 
9. Inception 2010
10. Good Luck, Have Fun, Don’t Die

## 5. Seeking hilarious Romcoms

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Hello! I truly need a good laugh, so I'm asking for Romcom suggestions. I'm open to any Era. I love Fun with Dick and Jane, Crazy Stupid Love, How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days, 50 First Dates, Just Go With It, Sleepless in Seattle, Forgetting Sarah Marshall, Office Space,   Click, etc...Thanks in advance for your recommendations!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Palm Springs
  - Palm Springs
2. The Proposal 
3. Mr. Right
4. The Wedding Singer 
5. Some Like It Hot

10 Things I Hate About You

Bottoms
6. Family Man, When Harry Met Sally, 10 Things I Hate About You, Always Be My Maybe 
7. High Fidelity
8. The wedding singer and Love Actually
9. IQ

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Happy 93rd birthday to Sir Michael Caine!! Do you have a favorite character he played?

**Score:** 1275
**Body:** I’ll start. Alfred Pennyworth in The Dark Knight trilogy 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Old hippy in Children Of Men, Jasper! 

*Pull my finger!*
2. Lawrence Jameson in Dirty Rotten Scoundrels 
3. Alfred (Dark Knight Trilogy) 
4. Miss Congeniality

![gif](giphy|sVY5o5OcLBDtm)
5. It’s not my favourite, but the first film of his that I ever saw was “Secondhand Lions.” I loved that movie, and it still has a fond place in my memory. 
6. Austin Powers’ dad
  - >There's only two things I hate in this world: people who are intolerant of other people's cultures, and the Dutch

The way he says it always makes me laugh.
7. Ebenezer Scrooge in *The Muppet Christmas Carol*.

![gif](giphy|YkOGa9K7gc3g5u7FXF)
8. Zulu
9. Charlie Crocker in The Italian Job (1969). If you haven’t seen the original, don’t hold the remake against it. 

## 2. An average actor who keep topping highest paid actors list

**Score:** 352

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|J53wyiG57vjm6RTqnz|downsized)
  - My least favorite thing is when he’s in movies that don’t do well for a bit then comes back to ruin WWE for 6 months. 
2. Shooter is one of my guilty pleasures.
  - Idc what anyone says, Shooter is an awesome movie.
    - I’m also a fan of Four Brothers

I don’t know why, I just like it. Maybe it’s the soundtrack. 
  - Bob Lee Swagger! A character name I’ll never forget 💪 
3. He was great playing a Boston asshole in The Departed.  

That’s the only time I enjoyed watching him act
  - He wasn’t acting 
4. Shooter, Lone Survivor, The Departed, The Italian Job, Boogie Nights and a few other hits. I think he’s done okay for himself irrespective of his acting skills 
5. Hes a terrible actor and a terrible person. Unfortunately hes in movies I enjoy, like the other guys.

![gif](giphy|1GgYiIZsoB6s8|downsized)

## 3. As a Dad I cry at movies all the time.... what scene gets your water works going?

**Score:** 353
**Body:** Toy Story 3 incinerator scene where they just accept their fate...

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bing Bong from inside Out


The Grave of the Fireflies, 97% of the movie
2. “Why isn’t he wearing his glasses? He can’t see without his glasses?
3. When The Iron Giant decides to save the town
  - "I go. You stay. No following"

Still probably my favorite Vin Diesel role, Mainly because it was one of my son's favorite films when he was wee
4. Can’t make it through the first 5 minutes of Up without being gutted. 
5. Almost the entire 2nd half of Coco.
6. When Rylie hugs it out with her parents in Inside Out about being sad and missing Minnesota. When that core memory drops after the sigh it was water work city lol
7. ![gif](giphy|cHkA7tJZoWLyU)

The only one
8. In the Lion King when Simba finds Mufasa’s dead body and keeps trying to wake him up until he finally realizes that he is dead and starts to cry. 
9. ![gif](giphy|fdoXPPhQImo9y)

This scene in Armageddon. Even still makes an old male friend of mine cry.

## 4. Antony Starr as Homelander is one of the most malicious, twisted TV villains ever

**Score:** 307
**Body:** Rewatched the entirety of The Boys to prepare for the finale and I’m appalled at just how monstrous his character is. 

One of the most evil villains on TV ever. Just a ruthless psychopath. The airplane sequence being a prime example of what a fiend he is. 

Antony Starr is phenomenal in the role. Dominates the screen like no other. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He's really underrated actor in that show. 
  - He is a phenomenal actor.

Honestly? He deserves an Emmy nomination for this. That’s how good he is. 
    - Also great in Banshee
2. No, he is just a fucking disappointment.

![gif](giphy|q9ldmTuld2Vbifpp4W)


  - Ouch!

That line hurts. Also Jensen Ackles as Soldier Boy is too good. 
3. The first episode is so amazing. When you suddenly see the glowing eyes outside of the plane... and its the kid who is happy to see Homelander.... damn. Perfect reveal 
4. I unironically miss all of the Antony Starr homelander memes and threads. He was the most talked about character in pop culture for a hot minute. I forgot about The Boys that last season was such ass. 
5. I’ve never wanted a character to die as much as I have with Homelander. Antony’s played him to perfection 🔥
6. I'm waiting for his death in the finale season.  Been waiting for his death for a long time.  I don't really care who does it at this point.  
  - Hope it’s Butcher. 

He deserves to finally take him down. 

## 5. A 90s movie that u recently watched and loved

**Score:** 171

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “I’m the bad guy?”
2. I recently watched this as well. I used it in a video montage I made for a college film class not long after it came out and I saw it through different eyes at 50 than I did at 18.
3. This is one of Michael Douglas best movies. 
4. I like the secene with the two gay men in the army/navy surplus store and the owner tells them to get out so they knock over a rack and call him a fascist lol
5. Captain Ron
6. Dude just wanted breakfast after 10:30 am. I get that
7. https://preview.redd.it/6g7uab8d52pg1.jpeg?width=1710&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=cfd1f50fb30052c9a44952434a50328b2ce4c84d
8. Princess Mononoke. 
9. Rewatched the Boondock Saints last night actually. Still one of my all time favs
10. Run Lola Run


I'd heard a lot about it, but had never seen it. Unfortunately I didn't get to see it in the original German.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. "Zap / You're Pregnant / That's Witchcraft." in experimental filmmaker Kenneth Anger's short film a point about Cinema

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I sat down and watched a few of Kenneth Anger's experimental film shorts from the 1940-1960s, because I was researching his possible influence on David Lynch, and though some were very beautiful (Scorpio Rising, Fireworks), what stays with me is the above line at the ending of his 1967 occult-grounded, surrealistic Invocation of My Demon Brother, ["Zap / You're Pregnant / That's Witchcraft"](https://youtu.be/unWaJp2zah8?si=qMMiK3gxN5b7e1g2&t=640) Not a fan of the film, but the message at the end gave insight to what he imagined film could do, as a pioneer in the art, something I believe David Lynch felt too, that film, in a kind of magic, a literal magic, can impregnate you with ideas, thoughts and dreams, and once the film is over, the magical spell has already happened, you leave the theater with something new growing in you.

Over time the idea has reached well past David Lynch thoughts, and really seems to describe the inexorable power of great films, perhaps better than any other concept. Films like Sorcerer, or Three Women, or Purple Noon, or Bring Me the Head of Alfredo Garcia, or Leave Her to Heaven, or supreme classics like Vertigo or 2001: A Space Odyssey, or Andrei Rublev, Sunset Boulevard have this way of impacting you where you say: How did you do that? How did you make me feel or think these things? It is a literal kind of magic. The cumulative effect of moving images, character, story, edits, sound, dialogue, camera/lens characteristics, lighting, facializations, action come together with an untraceable power.

There is of course all kinds of uses and effects of cinema, from entertainment and amusement to serious intellectual provocation, but more and more I'm drawn to cinema that feels like that, that when the film is done there's a mysterious "zap" that has happened. I do enjoy lots of films that don't have that feeling, good films, but less and less do I want to rewatch them.

Cinema as a sort of public dreaming.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 2. Undertone can't decide which of 50 different horror movies it's actually trying to be, so it settles on being nothing at all. (minor spoilers)

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Sometimes I'll get out of a movie that I don't think is strictly speaking good, but I enjoyed the process of watching a film fail in interesting ways, fail because its bold choices didn't pay off the way the director hoped. Undertone is the opposite of that. Undertone is a maddeningly boring, long, slow failure.

Undertone is a movie that has seen every other horror movie, and learned the language of the genre to perfect fluency. It's full of dark doorways in the background of shots. It's full of children's songs sung creepily. It's full of noises without clear sources. It's full of shots framed to be full of threatening shadows for extended stretches. It's dripping with religious guilt. It's got the fear of losing a mother, and the fear of becoming one. It's bursting with the fear of failure and inadequacy. It's jammed with haunted houses and demonic possession and flickering lights and child sacrifice. It's even got The Ring. 

But it's about absolutely none of these things, it merely uses these elements as set dressing. Our poorly defined protagonist who we're told is a hard nosed skeptic but takes the ghost stories she reads at face value wanders from room to room, scene to scene, snapping her head to look into empty dark corners full of nothing. It's just exhausting. Nothing has any payoff. In the climax it almost decides to settle on being Silent Hill 2 (the game) but then basically immediately abandons that extremely poorly built revelation, letting it disappear into its unfocused, glacially paced haze of horror tropes as suddenly as it emerged.

It's an ultimately hollow, pointless film that feels like if it bolts enough elements of really effective horror movies to itself that it will also become an effective horror movie, but this cargo-cult approach to horror can't hide the glaring fact that it has no connective tissue, poorly built characters, and no idea what story it's actually trying to tell. For all its mastery of the language of horror, it has nothing to say.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sounds kinda like a Shelby Oaks situation. Valid to want to pay homage to the movies that inspired you, but that can't be *all* you have going for you, and you definitely can spread yourself too thin with it. You need to carve out your own identity, or at the very least commit to exploring one subgenre well rather than several poorly 
2. That's probably due to the epidemic of filmmakers who, because they're terrified of being preachy, completely abandon any concept of Theme, and therefore the films are about nothing. For all of their "film knowledge," they don't know Story structure. That's why so many movies feel pointless and are instantly forgettable.

The converse of that is what John Truby teaches in The Anatomy of Genres, that Genres aren't types of movies, they're Theme delivery systems. And Horror is one of the most fundamental.

## 3. Roger & Me (1989)

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Directed by Michael Moore

Michael Moore's first film has a rather simple premise, to secure an interview with Roger Smith, the president of General Motors, to discuss the factory closures in Flint, Michigan, the city where Moore grew up. This quest to find the person responsible for the unemployment of an entire city is the driving force of the documentary.

Between offices, public events, and clubs, Moore tries unsuccessfully to approach Smith. The search for the company president serves as a thread that organizes the narrative, but the true significance lies elsewhere, as the camera simultaneously focuses on Flint and the consequences of the factory closures. We see a city devastated by unemployment, people evicted from their homes, businesses shuttered, people leaving the city, and the abandonment by national authorities who seem to have no solution. Moore creates a rather interesting character (himself), as he is neither an invisible narrator nor a mere observer. He is the character who persists in asking questions and in trying to get an interview he will likely never obtain. This insistence helps the structure, as if it were a story about someone determined to achieve something the system has designed to prevent.

Despite the crisis, Moore managed to create a portrait of the absurdity that capitalism can reach. The poverty and violence that begin to engulf Flint must coexist with extravagant (and expensive) initiatives to "revitalize" the city, entrepreneurs who promise hope to the unemployed, and those convinced that the problem is that people don't want to work. Many of the harshest scenes are conveyed in a humorous tone, as if the only way to confront certain situations were by pointing out how ridiculous they are. However, the laughter it provokes is awkward, as it often precedes or follows very sad moments.

The film, and Michael Moore's filmography in general, has been the subject of discussion regarding its presentation of events. In this case, they point out that the montage doesn't correspond to the actual chronology, but what's being attempted here is a commentary on a problem rather than an exact reconstruction of the events. It doesn't aim to be a neutral report, it's an intervention that takes a side and builds its argument from indignation and irony.

**MINOR SPOILER**

In the end, Moore never gets the interview he's after, and that absence ends up speaking louder than any possible answer. The GM president is unavailable anywhere they try to reach him, and when confronted, he avoids being questioned and discussing the issue. There are decisions that can completely transform the life of an entire city, and the people who make them rarely have the courage or the concern to look those who pay the price in the eye.

[Letterboxd (review in Spanish)](https://boxd.it/dtE1sv)

[Substack (English and Spanish)](https://substack.com/@cristologia?r=2as6ge&utm_medium=ios&utm_source=stories&shareImageVariant=light)

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. mulholland drive is peak cinema

**Score:** 12
**Body:** the greatest dreamy neo noir mystery movie i’ve ever seen.

amazing cinematography. phenomenal acting, beautiful soundtrack. 

now about the movie 

although i know you can interpret this in so many ways the way i saw it was that its diane’s dream/harsh reality she wish she had and how this is the final dream she has (the pillow scene in the beginning) before she commits suicide (shown in the end). david lynch portrays this film as a dream so well . simple takes become possible. nobody can articulate themselves properly anymore. the side missions get you side tracked. 

the characters go along with it aswell like how it occurs in a dream and nobody decides to question anything until they’ve woken up. the acting is very hammy at first but after you realize it was a dream it all makes sense. 

one of the best things about this film was that everything in the dream contributed to something in the real world-

my favourite scene was betty’s audition. the acting by naomi watts is phenomenal, compared to her acting before which again adds up to why it was more of a dream. 

the film has its humorous parts like the cowboy and his dialogues.  

and not to mention the fucking creepy parts which geniunely gave me chills. 

overall this was an amazing film one of my favourites already although i’d have to rewatch it. 

A man's attitude goes some ways, the way his life will be

also please someone tell me the meanings of the flairs in this subreddit

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Possibly my favourite film ever. I watched this at the age of 16 and was amazed to find myself so moved and enthralled by a film where I didnt know what the hell was going on at all. 

Against some other peoples advice i went to the internet to look theories and explainations and actually that helped me appreciate the film even further and helped me understand at that age the concept of allegory within film.
2. I've always cited this as possibly my favorite movie of all time. Definitely Top 10 and most likely top 5. From the dreamlike confusion of the first viewing, to the deep dive into reading about the story and interpretations, to the absolutely perfect soundtrack. My first Lynch movie, and started my journey to watch everything he'd ever done, and it's never disappointed.
3. I show this movie to everyone I possibly can. I know it isn’t for everyone but it really gets the point across that you can break a lot of “traditional” rules in movies and end up with a masterpiece. 
4. Top 5 ever for me and it’s probably not 5. The way it makes you feel at an absolute visceral level is just unparalleled. The true dream state that you never remember when you wake up, yet translated perfectly to film. How could he remember?  Can we remember?  

## 5. New zombie documentary Black Zombie

**Score:** 6
**Body:** I had the chance to see the new documentary *Black Zombie* as part of South by Southwest. I found it intriguing, and I'm really hoping it finds a streamer and broader distribution. Yes, it addresses *Night of the Living Dead*, but more than that, it's about the zombie's role in Haitian culture. This has been covered somewhat in the documentary *Horror Noire: A History of Black Horror*, but as far as I know, we haven't yet had a documentary that fully explores the zombie's evolution from Haiti to Hollywood. I found it fascinating, especially the interviews with voodoo practitioners and how the doc analyzes voodoo's anti-colonial history.

Even if you don't necessarily like horror, I still recommend seeing this when/if it lands on a streamer or plays at a local arthouse theater. Anyway, here is a longer review, if anyone is interested. I also had a chance to chat with the director, Maya Annik Bedward.

[Black Zombie explores the monster's Haitian roots and evolution](https://www.thehorrorlounge.com/post/black-zombie-explores-the-monster-s-haitian-roots-and-evolution-to-a-hollywood-fixture)

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)
